title: FID Magnetometer Based on Paraffin-Coated Planar Reflective Multipass Cells
authors: Xiangyu Li, Tingxuan Guo, Yang Li, Kaifeng Zhao
abstract: We demonstrate a paraffin-coated planar reflective multipass vapor cell for compact optical atomic magnetometry. The cell has an internal volume of $12 \times 12 \times 8~\mathrm{mm}^3$ and supports 20 optical passes with a total transmittance exceeding $65\%$, while maintaining a longitudinal spin-relaxation time of $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$ longer than $1~\mathrm{s}$. The planar geometry provides spatially separated input and output beams, enabling compact optical integration. A single-cell free-induction-decay (FID) magnetometer reaches $10~\mathrm{pT}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ in the geomagnetic-field range, presently limited by current-source noise in the field coils. A two-cell differential configuration achieves a sensitivity of $\sim 28~\mathrm{fT}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ over the $1$--$15~\mathrm{Hz}$ band for bias fields of $0.3$--$0.7~\mathrm{G}$. These results establish that paraffin-coated planar multipass cells offer high optical depth, long coherence times, and an integration-friendly platform for ultrasensitive magnetometry.
summary: The paper presents a new design for a planar, multi-pass vapor cell using paraffin coatings and integrated mirrors to create a compact, high-sensitivity magnetometer. By using a differential two-cell configuration, the researchers achieved femtotesla-scale sensitivity. This approach provides a scalable platform for ultra-sensitive applications in biomagnetism and aerospace.
main_problem: The challenge of developing compact, high-sensitivity optical atomic magnetometers (OAMs) that maintain long coherence times and high signal-to-noise ratios while reducing sensor volume.
main_result: Demonstration of a planar reflective multipass vapor cell that achieves a differential gradiometer sensitivity of approximately 28 fT/sqrt(Hz) in the geomagnetic field range.
method: The researchers used a paraffin-coated borosilicate glass cell with integrated dielectric mirrors to create a 20-pass optical trajectory, employing Free Induction Decay (FID) magnetometry with a two-cell differential configuration.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of a $^{87}$Rb vapor cell (12 x 12 x 8 mm$^3$) with an anti-relaxation paraffin coating and integrated $	ext{SiO}_2/	ext{Ta}_2	ext{O}_5$ dielectric mirrors, operated in a magnetic gradiometer setup within a $\mu$-metal shield.
key_observables: Larmor frequency, longitudinal spin-relaxation time ($T_1$), transverse relaxation rate ($\Gamma_2$), and magnetic-field sensitivity.
important_parameters: 20 optical passes, total transmittance > 65%, $T_1 > 1$ s, 1-15 Hz bandwidth, and sensitivity limits including photon shot-noise (2.9 fT/sqrt(Hz)) and spin-projection noise (6.5 fT/sqrt(Hz)).
main_assumptions: Neglecting longitudinal relaxation during the probing interval and treating correlations between projection noise at the start and end of measurement as negligible.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the cell fabrication schematic and multipass beam geometry; Figure 2 displays photographs of the cell and infrared images of resonant fluorescence along the optical path; Figure 7 presents the sensitivity measurement of the gradiometer compared to the frequency-counter noise floor.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the need for compact magnetometers, describes the fabrication and coating of the planar multipass cell, details the experimental magnetometry setup and noise analysis, and concludes with the demonstration of high-sensitivity gradiometry.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how engineered cell geometries and coatings can control spin relaxation and decoherence, pushing the limits of sensitivity in atomic ensembles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fabrication and optical characterization of the pla- nar reflective multipass vapor cell. Left: Schematic illustra- tion of the coated-window assembly joined by optical contact bonding. The two diagonally positioned circles indicate the cell stems. Right: Multipass beam geometry. Upper right: Infrared image of the transmitted beam spots with ten re- flections from the back window (corresponding to 20 optical passes). The spot size increases for later passes because of beam divergence during propagation.

FIG. 2. Photographs and optical characterization of paraffin- coated planar multipass vapor cells. (a) Photograph of a com- pleted cell with internal dimensions of 12 mm × 8 mm(cross section)×12 mm(length) and a wall thickness of 2 mm. (b) Infrared image showing resonant fluorescence along the opti- cal beam path inside the cell, corresponding to 19 reflections and 20 light passes.

FIG. 3. Temperature dependence of the longitudinal relax- ation time T1 for two paraffin-coated planar multipass vapor cells. The inset shows an exponential fit to the longitudinal spin relaxation of cell B at 50 ◦C.

FIG. 4. Schematic of the experimental setup. NPBS: non- polarizing beam splitter; CL: convex Lens; PBS: polarizing beam splitter; OS: optical shutter; λ/4: quarter-wave plate; λ/2: half-wave plate. A Barker coil system on the inner cylin- der of the magnetic shield (not shown) generates a static mag- netic field along the z axis. A separate pair of Helmholtz coils produces an rf magnetic field along the x axis.

FIG. 5. Control timing sequence. The pump duration, rf pulse duration, and frequency-counter gate time are 2.4 ms, 0.1 ms, and 27.5 ms, respectively, resulting in a total mea- surement cycle of 33 ms. The actual repetition period is 3 ms longer than the nominal sum of the pulse durations. This difference arises because only part of the FID signal is used for counter-based frequency extraction, and finite delays are inserted between pulses to ensure proper timing synchroniza- tion.
